---
title: "Piping in Python"
description: "Simplified data pipelines."
#author: "Martin Stoffel"
date: "01/02/2023"
categories: [Python, code]
image: pipe.png
draft: true
---

```{r, include = FALSE}
library(reticulate)
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  #collapse = TRUE,
  comment = "#>"
)
```

In R, we might do data-wrangling like this:

```{r}
library(dplyr)

# load the iris dataset, filter, select, group, summarise

iris %>%
  filter(Species == "setosa") %>%
  select(Sepal.Length, Sepal.Width) %>%
  group_by(Sepal.Length) %>%
  summarise(mean_width = mean(Sepal.Width))
```

The usual way in Python's Pandas is much less elegant, as it requires a lot of intermediate variables:

```{python}
import pandas as pd

# load the iris dataset, filter, select, group, summarise

iris = pd.read_csv("https://raw.githubusercontent.com/mwaskom/seaborn-data/master/iris.csv")
iris_setosa = iris[iris.Species == "setosa"]
iris_setosa = iris_setosa[["Sepal.Length", "Sepal.Width"]]
iris_setosa = iris_setosa.groupby("Sepal.Length")
iris_setosa = iris_setosa.mean()
iris_setosa
```

But we can actually pipe in Python, too! Something like this:

```{python}
import pandas as pd

# load the iris dataset, filter, select, group, summarise

(iris
  .query("Species == 'setosa'")
  .loc[:, ['Sepal.Length', 'Sepal.Width']]
  .groupby('Sepal.Length')
  .agg({'Sepal.Width': 'mean'})
)
```

Some functions don't natively work in a pipe, but we can use the `pipe` function to make them work. For example, the renaming function `rename` doesn't work in a pipe, but we can use `pipe` to make it work:

```{python}

# load the iris dataset, filter, select, group, summarise

(iris
  .pipe(lambda df: df[df.Species == "setosa"])
  .pipe(lambda df: df[["Sepal.Length", "Sepal.Width"]])
  .pipe(lambda df: df.groupby("Sepal.Length"))
  .pipe(lambda df: df.mean())
)
```

If you'd like something just like R, there is a package called 'siuba' that is a port of the 'dplyr' package to Python. It's not as mature as 'dplyr' yet, but it's getting there. You can install it with `pip install siuba` and then use it like this:

```{python}
import pandas as pd
from siuba import _, filter, select, group_by, summarize

# load the iris dataset, filter, select, group, summarise

(iris
  >> filter(_.Species == "setosa")
  >> select(_.Sepal.Length, _.Sepal.Width)
  >> group_by(_.Sepal.Length)
  >> summarize(mean_width = _.Sepal.Width.mean())
)
```


Nice! We can see all of this next to each other in a table:

| R | Python | Python (pipe) | Python (siuba) |
|---|--------|---------------|----------------|
| iris %>% filter(Species == "setosa") %>% select(Sepal.Length, Sepal.Width) %>% group_by(Sepal.Length) %>% summarise(mean_width = mean(Sepal.Width)) | iris[iris.Species == "setosa"][["Sepal.Length", "Sepal.Width"]].groupby("Sepal.Length").mean() | (iris .query("Species == 'setosa'") .loc[:, ['Sepal.Length', 'Sepal.Width']] .groupby('Sepal.Length') .agg({'Sepal.Width': 'mean'})) | (iris >> filter(_.Species == "setosa") >> select(_.Sepal.Length, _.Sepal.Width) >> group_by(_.Sepal.Length) >> summarize(mean_width = _.Sepal.Width.mean())) |

I hope this helps you to write more elegant Python code!
